title: Detecting Objects in Video
concept: Video object detection localizes objects in every frame of a video, leveraging temporal context to handle motion blur and occlusions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main drawback of Two-Stream networks in real-time deployment?

options.text: They require expensive real-time optical flow computation
They can only output classification labels for static pictures
They cannot use transfer learning features

exercises.explain: Correct! Calculating optical flow on the fly is extremely CPU/GPU intensive, hindering real-time performance.

cards.title: Spotting the blur
cards.body: When you pause a video of a racing car, the image is often a blurry streak of color. A standard 2D object detector might completely miss it because it doesn't look like a normal car. But because we know it was a car in the previous frames, we can use history to solve the blur!

cards.title: Video Object Detection
cards.body: Video Object Detection is the task of identifying and localizing (with bounding boxes) objects in every frame of a video. Unlike static image detection, video detectors use temporal context from nearby frames to improve accuracy.

cards.title: An attentive security guard
cards.body: Imagine a guard watching a gate. A visitor walks behind a pillar. A static image detector forgets the person exists the moment they are hidden. A temporal video detector remembers the person walked behind the pillar and expects them to emerge on the other side.

cards.title: Handling occlusion
cards.body: In self-driving cars, a pedestrian might be temporarily blocked by a trash can. By leveraging memory of the pedestrian's previous position and velocity vector, the detector maintains a bounding box over them even while blocked.

cards.title: Not just 2D detector loop
cards.body: You might think video detection is just running YOLO on every frame in a loop. While simple, that ignores temporal context! Real video detectors link feature maps across time, using past frames to help classify blurry or partially blocked objects.

cards.title: 2D vs. Video Object Detection
cards.body: Comparing static and temporal object detection methods:

left.text:
Detects on independent frames
Struggles with blur and occlusion
No historical context

right.text:
Links features across frames
Handles blur using temporal context
Tracks objects across occlusions

exercises.prompt: How does video object detection handle motion blur?

options.text: By using temporal context and features from surrounding clear frames
By increasing the contrast of the blur manually
By converting all video frames to vectors
By ignoring all moving objects completely

exercises.explain: Correct! Temporal context from adjacent frames helps resolve details that are blurry in a single frame.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An object detector that operates on individual frames without memory is vulnerable to occlusion.
exercises.explain: True. If an object is hidden behind a wall, a memoryless detector cannot detect it, whereas a video detector can use history.

exercises.sentence: Video object detectors improve robustness by using temporal ___ from adjacent frames.
exercises.explain: Temporal context allows information from previous and future frames to support the current frame's prediction.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a typical challenge that temporal video detectors solve?

options.text: Low audio volume in the recording

exercises.explain: Temporal detection helps maintain object localization even during temporary occlusion.

exercises.prompt: Why is running a standard 2D YOLO model frame-by-frame on a video suboptimal for a security system?

options.text: Because it fails to utilize temporal links, causing bounding boxes to flicker or disappear when objects are partially blocked.
Because YOLO only works on static images and cannot open video files.
Because 2D detectors cannot detect human shapes.
Because it requires decompressing the video to analog tapes.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Without temporal memory, if a person walks behind a lamp post, the box disappears and reappears, causing flicker. Next: how do we enforce consistency over time?
